metadata.title: Write the 500-Word Article
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 9 brief — Making Your Brand Unforgettable

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 4 of 6. Part 2 of the assignment is a live published article worth forty points. Not a draft, not a PDF — a piece that exists at a real URL. This episode builds the 500-word article. The arc from Episode 2 is the backbone, the framework is visible in the structure, and the formatting makes it scannable enough that a stranger will read to the end. Path A leads with personal expertise; Path B makes the reader care about a problem first. Either way, the article ends with a clear call to action. Let's go.

props.command: 500+ words. Framework visible in structure. Headline + subheads + short paragraphs + CTA. Path A (expertise) or Path B (problem-first).
props.runningText: a piece that exists at a real URL — not a draft, not a PDF…
output: ~500 words with the arc visible in the structure
Clear CTA at the end — what the reader does next

beats.act: two paths — which structure to use
beats.narration_text: Two paths for the article structure, corresponding to the brand path from Episode 1. Path A is a personal brand article. Lead with a specific POV or a professional expertise statement — not a bio, a perspective. 'After building three AI pipelines that produced output no human reviewed before it reached users, I stopped trusting any pipeline I did not write a failure mode for first.' That is a lead. It states a position, it implies experience, and it makes a reader want to know what the failure mode was. Develop with the 300-word story from Episode 3 as the body — the arc is already there. Weave the Madison tool in naturally, as evidence of the expertise, not as an advertisement. Position as a practitioner worth following: what do you know that a peer at your stage does not? Path B is a startup brand article. Lead with the problem — make the reader feel the user's pain before you describe the tool. 'Every marketing analyst I interviewed spent forty minutes a day sorting news articles by hand, looking for the three stories their director actually needed.' That is the reader's problem, stated with specificity. Build to the value proposition: here is what the tool does, here is what changes. Show a before-and-after — what the user experienced before and after the tool existed. End with a clear call to action.

artifactLines: Path A: lead with a specific POV · body = 300-word story · weave tool in as evidence · position as practitioner.
Lead test: does it state a position + imply experience + make a reader want to know more?
Path B: lead with the user's pain (specific + stated with numbers) · build to value prop · before/after · CTA.
Lead test: does the reader feel the problem before you describe the solution?
Same path as A6/A7. Keep the brand consistent across the series.
props.sparkLine: Path A: what you know. Path B: what the user feels. Pick one. Lead with it.

beats.narration_text: An article that is not scannable is an article that is not read. Most people on LinkedIn Articles and Medium scan before they commit — they read the headline, the subheadings, and the first sentence of a few paragraphs. If those three things do not earn their attention, they leave. The formatting rules. A headline: specific, not clever. Not 'Lessons From My Journey' — 'Why I Stopped Trusting AI Output That Nobody Reviewed Before It Shipped.' The headline should tell a reader what the article is about and why it matters to them, without being a summary. Subheadings every one hundred to one hundred fifty words — named after the section's content, not generic. Not 'The Problem' — 'Forty Minutes a Day, Every Day, Looking for Three Stories.' Short paragraphs: three to four sentences maximum. A five-sentence paragraph that could be two paragraphs should be two paragraphs. On mobile, which is where most LinkedIn readers are, a five-sentence paragraph is a wall. And no bold words or italics scattered through the text unless they carry real weight — not formatting decoration.

artifactLines: Headline: specific, not clever — tells what + why it matters. Not 'Lessons From My Journey.'
Subheads every 100–150 words — named after content, not 'The Problem' or 'The Solution.'
Paragraphs: 3–4 sentences max. 5-sentence paragraph = wall on mobile = reader leaves.
No decoration bold/italics — only when the word carries real weight.
Scan test: read headline + subheads only. Does the article still make sense?
props.sparkLine: Headline + subheads + first sentences = the reader's scan path. Earn it.

beats.narration_text: The call to action. Every article ends with one — a specific sentence that tells the reader what to do next. Not 'I hope this was helpful.' Not 'follow me for more content.' A CTA that is connected to the article's content and the reader's situation. The best CTAs are one of three types. The question: 'If you are building a pipeline right now — what is the one failure mode you have not defined yet?' It is a direct question that makes the reader think about their own situation. The invitation: 'I am documenting every quality gate decision for this pipeline as I build it — if you want to compare notes, connect with me.' It is specific about what you are doing and what the connection would be about. The resource: 'The brand_config.json template I use for every new pipeline is in my GitHub — link in the comments.' It is something the reader can take. The CTA to avoid: 'If you found this valuable, please like and subscribe.' This sounds like an algorithm, not a person. The assignment brief says the article ends with a clear CTA. Write it last, after you know what the article actually says, because the CTA has to be earned by the content — not bolted on.

artifactLines: Question CTA: ask the reader about their own situation — makes them think, not just consume.
Invitation CTA: specific about what you're doing + what the connection would be about.
Resource CTA: something the reader can take — template, link, doc, tool.
Avoid: 'If you found this valuable, please like and subscribe' = algorithm voice, not a person.
Write the CTA last — after you know what the article says. It must be earned by the content.
props.sparkLine: CTA must be earned by the content. Write it last. Specific, not algorithmic.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for the 500-Word Article. Path A leads with expertise — a specific POV, then the 300-word story as the body, the tool woven in as evidence. Path B leads with the user's problem — stated specifically with numbers, built to the value proposition, before-and-after, then the CTA. Both paths: a specific headline that earns the click, subheadings every one hundred to one hundred fifty words named after content not sections, paragraphs of three to four sentences, and a CTA connected to the article's content — question, invitation, or resource. The CTA is written last. The article is a draft until it is published at a real URL. Episode 5 handles the graphic, the publish, and the promo.

artifactLines: Path A: expertise lead · 300-word story body · tool as evidence · practitioner positioning.
Path B: problem lead (specific + numbers) · value prop · before/after · CTA.
Format: specific headline · subheads every 100–150 words · 3–4 sentence paragraphs.
CTA: question / invitation / resource — not 'like and subscribe.' Written last.
Draft until published at a real URL. Episode 5 = graphic + publish + promo.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the article-building prompt. Paste it into Claude and fill in the brackets. The prompt reads: I am writing a 500-word article for publication on LinkedIn Articles or Medium. I am taking Path [A or B]. My article structure is: Path A — lead with this expertise statement or POV: [one to two sentences about what I know from building the pipeline]. Body: my 300-word story [paste it]. Closing: how the tool or approach demonstrates this expertise. CTA: [draft of the question, invitation, or resource I want to end with]. OR Path B — lead with this problem statement: [one to two sentences describing the user's pain, with specific numbers if I have them]. Value proposition: what the tool does. Before and after: [bullet points for before state and after state]. CTA: [draft of the question, invitation, or resource I want to end with]. Now write the full article: one headline using this structure — specific, not clever — four to six subheadings named after the content of each section, paragraphs of three to four sentences, and the CTA as the final paragraph. Total five hundred words, plus or minus fifty. After you get the draft, run the scan test: read only the headline and the subheadings. Does the article still make sense? If the subheadings are generic like 'the problem' or 'the solution,' rewrite them to name the content. Then apply the voice pass from Episode 3 before you publish.

props.command: Path A: expertise lead [1–2 sentences] · 300-word story [paste] · tool as evidence · CTA draft. OR Path B: problem lead [specific + numbers] · value prop · before/after bullets · CTA draft. Build: 1 headline + 4–6 content-named subheads + 3–4 sentence paragraphs + CTA. ~500 words.
props.runningText: scan test after draft: headline + subheads only — if they're generic, rewrite them to name the content…

beats.narration_text: Write the 500-Word Article. Liam, in for Bear.
